content:
First, note that  
\(S = 9 + 9^2 + \cdots + 9^{2010} = 9\,\dfrac{9^{2010}-1}{9-1} \;=\;\dfrac{9^{2011}-9}{8}\).  

We check small primes \(p\) in turn:

1. \(p=2\): each \(9^k\equiv1\pmod2\), so \(S\equiv2010\cdot1\equiv0\pmod2\).  
2. \(p=3\): \(9\equiv0\pmod3\), so \(S\equiv0\pmod3\).  
3. \(p=5\): \(9\equiv4\pmod5\), cycle of \(4^k\) mod 5 has period 2, and there are 1005 full periods in 2010 terms, giving total \(\equiv0\pmod5\).  
4. \(p=7,11,13\): similarly one checks that the order of 9 mod \(p\) divides 2010, so \(S\equiv0\pmod p\).  
5. \(p=17\): here \(9\pmod{17}\) has order 8, but \(8\nmid2010\).  In fact  
   \(\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k\equiv\) (251 full 8‐cycles)\(\times0\;+\;(9^1+9^2)\equiv9+13\equiv5\pmod{17}\neq0.\)

Thus the smallest prime that does *not* divide \(S\) is 

\(\boxed{17}.\)

4. \(p=7,11,13\): similarly one checks that the order of 9 mod \(p\) divides 2010, so \(S\equiv0\pmod p\).  
5. \(p=17\): here \(9\pmod{17}\) has order 8, but \(8\nmid2010\).  In fact

\(\boxed{17}.\)
